steps.text: Determine whether the licensee’s fuel handling procedures are adequate to perform intended functions.
Determine whether fuel is moved and inspected consistent with the requirements of the TS and the licensee’s procedures.
Determine whether fuel handling activities are conducted in accordance with 10 CFR Part 20 and the licensee’s procedures and programs for radiation protection.
Determine whether the licensee satisfied security plan requirements for fuel movement activities.
Determine whether significant fuel movement or inspection problems are identified and resolved in accordance with the licensee’s procedural controls.
Determine whether the licensee was within TS limits and met procedural requirements before resuming normal operation after fuel movement.
Be aware of the facility’s plans and schedules for refueling or other major fuel movement.

Review the safety analysis report (SAR) or as-built facility descriptions, the TS, the licensee’s procedure(s), and the Security Plan for fuel design information, fuel movement methods, and requirements.
During the onsite inspection, be alert for significant differences in the SAR, or the TS descriptions of fuel and equipment as compared to the actual fuel and equipment.
Ensure design change review and approval is performed in accordance with 10 CFR 50.59 and the licensee’s procedures.
Verify if differences found are reflected in the as-built description of the facility.
Refer to ANSI/ANS-15.2 and Regulatory Guide 2.3 for guidance on new, plate-type fuel.
Refer to ANSI/ANS Standards listed in Appendix B to Inspection Procedure 69001 for general guidance.
Refer to Division 2 Regulatory Guides and other Regulatory Guides of possible interest for additional general guidance.
Observe one fuel movement activity.
Verify the final fuel location, review respective records, and discuss with personnel involved in the most recent fuel movement activity.
Verify whether the final location of the fuel is consistent with that specified in the licensee's records.
Handle fuel during refueling, fuel shuffling, and fuel inspection.
Transfer fuel to storage pits, racks, or containers designed to prevent accidental criticality, or remove only one bundle at a time to be inspected underwater and replace before proceeding.
Calculate the minimum number of fuel bundles for a critical mass with optimum moderation, or separate the bundles by the use of fuel racks designed for this purpose.
Monitor changes in the level of neutron flux.
Perform 1/M calculations independently by more than one person.
Inspect fuel and control rods in accordance with the TS and procedures.
Investigate and resolve control rod failures to ensure they perform their intended function.
Inspect the removed neutron source for leakage paths such as splitting or pitting.
Observe radiological controls during one fuel movement activity.
Review radiation protection records and conduct discussions with responsible personnel for the most recent fuel movement activity.
Monitor or sample for airborne radioactive particulate matter.
Monitor exits from controlled areas in accordance with 10 CFR Part 20 and licensee procedural requirements.
Shield the detector area or extend the controlled area to a low radiation area.
Perform surveys for contamination in accordance with licensee procedures.
Check the work exit area for contamination when each person leaves.
Survey licensee personnel, tools, and equipment for contamination upon exit of the work area.
Monitor radiation areas in accordance with Subpart F of 10 CFR Part 20, the TS and the licensee’s procedural requirements.
Survey periodically to determine whether actual radiation levels are consistent with area radiation monitors.
Check direct reading dosimeters frequently.

Verify personnel monitoring devices (such as film badges, TLDs, direct reading dosimeters and extremity monitors) are worn correctly and are appropriate for expected radiation fields and the type of work to be done.
Observe security controls during one fuel movement activity.
Review records and discuss with responsible personnel for the most recent fuel movement activity.
Observe the resolution of one fuel movement problem.
Identify problem areas by reviewing the licensee's logs and records, and discussing with responsible personnel for the most recent fuel movement activity.
Verify that the licensee resolved identified problems to meet TS requirements.
Observe one reactor startup after fuel movement.
Verify calibration of control rods, and determination of excess reactivity and shutdown margin.

preconditions.text: If practical to review documents prior to the onsite inspection
If significant differences in the SAR or TS descriptions compared to actual fuel/equipment are identified
Direct observation of a fuel movement activity is not possible at the time of inspection.
Participants must be trained and understand their tasks.
Dry runs must be conducted for operations of potential safety concern or of an unusual nature, including discussion of actions needed to avert or mitigate accidents.
If an installed neutron source is removed during refueling
If a control rod failure is identified
Direct observation of radiological controls is not possible at the time of inspection.
Area radiation levels are so high that a difference of about 0.2 millirem per hour (mr/hr) cannot be detected.
People are continuously entering and exiting the area to observe the work.
Direct observation of fuel movement problem resolution is not possible.
Direct observation of reactor startup is not possible.
Detailed records of the tests and checks were not covered under the Inspection Procedure on 'Class I Research and Test Reactor Surveillance'.
Changes to core configuration were made.

constraints.text: Under no circumstances is the licensee to adjust schedules for these activities to fit the inspection schedule.
Reference to guidance is to aid in the technical evaluation of licensee programs and is not to be used as requirements unless the licensee has committed to them in writing.
The use of approved procedures are required in accordance with TS and security plan requirements.
Ensure that the fuel is not damaged, that accidental criticality does not occur, and that radiation and contamination hazards are minimized.
Fuel handling tools must be consistent with TS requirements and licensee procedures.
Fuel handling must be in accordance with the TS and applicable procedures.
Threat of inadvertent criticality: additional moderation may increase multiplication, and water replacing a removed fuel rod can add more reactivity than fuel removal takes away.
Four fuel bundles in a square matrix pose a criticality hazard.
Some vane-type control blades might fall out of the core if broken.
A failure in one control rod could indicate that another might fail by the same mechanism.
Fuel movement can cause significant radiological hazards. A single fuel element can easily emit radiation at levels above 100 rem per hour at one foot.
Maintain occupational doses as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA).
Grab sampling is to be performed in accordance with approved licensee procedures.
Grab samples may substitute for continuous air monitors to verify compliance with 10 CFR Part 20 and the TS.
10 CFR Part 20, Subpart J provides the posting and labeling requirements for several radiation areas.
10 CFR Part 20, Subpart F requires surveys and ANSI/ANS 15.11, 1993 provides additional guidance on Radioactive Contamination Monitoring.
Personnel dosimetry is required by 10 CFR 20.1502 and is to be used as specified by the licensee’s procedures.
New fuel is required to be kept in the security area and locked in a fuel vault or in some way secured.
